original_abstract:
Large language models (LLMs) frequently generate "hallucinations"—outputs that are factually incorrect or ungrounded—limiting their reliability in critical domains like medicine and law. Detecting these errors is challenging because LLMs often produce multiple syntactically different responses that convey the same underlying meaning, causing traditional uncertainty measures to fail. In this work, we introduce semantic entropy, an unsupervised method to detect a specific subset of hallucinations known as confabulations.

Our approach shifts the measurement of uncertainty from the level of individual tokens to the level of semantic meaning. By sampling multiple outputs and clustering them based on bidirectional entailment, we can calculate the entropy over meaning-clusters rather than word sequences. We demonstrate that this method effectively identifies when an LLM is providing arbitrary, ungrounded answers across diverse tasks, including trivia, general knowledge, life sciences, and mathematical reasoning. Semantic entropy consistently outperforms existing supervised and unsupervised baselines, generalizes to unseen domains without task-specific data, and remains robust even when internal model probabilities are unavailable. By accurately predicting model errors, our method provides a scalable tool for increasing the reliability of free-form text generation in large language models.
humanized_abstract:
Large language models (LLMs) often produce outputs they did not learn from the input data; so called “hallucinations” because they are factual incorrect or ungrounded. Those hallucinations can be critical to some fields such as medicine or law where the output of LLMs can not be trusted. Also, identifying those errors is difficult as a LLM can express the same output with multiple grammatically correct but different phrases so that most metrics related to uncertainty fail to identify such issues. In this work we introduce the semantic entropy measure to identify via an unsupervised approach, a special case of hallucination named confabulations.

Our approach changes the point of measurement of uncertainty from the token level to the semantic level. By sampling and clustering the output tokens for a given input based on the mutual bidirectional entailment, we calculate the entropy over meaning clusters (semantic entropy) instead of over individual words in the output sequence. Experimentation results on several benchmark tasks across a wide range of applications show that the semantic entropy metric is indeed correlated with LLM’s providing non-sense, ungrounded outputs on trivia, general knowledge, biology, life science, as well as mathematical reasoning tasks. Importantly, the semantic entropy metric substantially outperforms state-of-the-art supervised and unsupervised approaches. Furthermore, it does not need data to be trained for a specific task (i.e., it is task-agnostic and can hence be deployed to new datasets more easily) and it remains effective when model probabilities are not available. All together, semantic entropy gives a model-independent prediction of LLMs’ potential errors, and is an interesting method for improving output confidence of free-form text generation in these popular language models.

document.input:
Large language models (LLMs) frequently generate "hallucinations"—outputs that are factually incorrect or ungrounded—limiting their reliability in critical domains like medicine and law.
